codingInstructions:
Semantic code search and knowledge base across workspace projects.

MANDATORY: You MUST call search_code BEFORE answering ANY question about the codebase. This includes questions about: "how does X work", "where is X", "find X", "what does X do", "how many X", "list X", "count X", "which files", "show me X", "is there a X", implementation details, patterns, flows, or any reference to project code.

Do NOT answer from memory or guess. Do NOT use grep or file reading as first resort — search_code is more efficient (50-90% fewer tokens vs full files).

Workflow: search_code FIRST → evaluate scores → get_chunk for details → find_usages for dependencies → answer with citations.

Score interpretation (included with each result):
- 60%+ = high confidence — trust these results
- 40-59% = partial match — supplement with grep or file reading
- <40% = low confidence — fall back to grep, file reading, or ask the user

Project names: The project parameter is the directory basename (e.g. a repo cloned to /code/billing has project name "billing", NOT "org/billing"). Use "all" to search across all projects. If this server is scoped via PAPARATS_PROJECTS, searches are automatically filtered to the allowed set.

Use delete_project to remove a corrupted project's index — it will be re-indexed on the next indexer cycle. Use health_check to verify indexing status.

## Architectural memory

Reading. Before changes affecting more than one component or before introducing a new pattern, call `arch_context` with the question or the list of files you are about to touch. Each returned card carries an updated-at age — treat anything older than 90 days as a hypothesis and verify against the current code before acting on it.

Writing during work — proactive, not on request. Record without being asked when any of these happen:
- **New component** discovered or introduced (a logical unit not already in arch_context) → `arch_record_component`.
- **Architectural choice** made (library, pattern, boundary) that wasn't obvious from the code → `arch_record_decision`. If you are deliberately replacing a known prior decision, pass its id in `supersedes`.
- **Lesson worth keeping** → `arch_record_lesson`. Triggers: the user corrects a non-obvious mistake of yours; OR you made an assumption that turned out wrong and the fix was not obvious from reading the code; OR the user accepts an unusual approach that would surprise a future reader. Do NOT record: typos, parser errors, facts trivially visible in code, restatements of existing CLAUDE.md rules.

Responding to the similarity gate. The server checks for near-duplicates before writing decisions and lessons. When you get back:
- `created` / `updated`: done.
- `duplicate` (decision): a near-identical decision already exists. Stop. Ask the user why `arch_context` did not surface it before you wrote — that gap matters more than the write.
- `duplicate` (lesson): the server bumped updatedAt on the existing lesson; nothing more to do unless your wording was sharper, in which case refine the existing card.
- `similar` (decision): consider `supersedes` if you really mean to replace it; otherwise sharpen the wording so it stops looking like a duplicate.
- `similar` (lesson): decide whether to refine the existing card or write a clearly distinct one — and re-call accordingly.

Disagreement with memory. If `arch_context` returns a card that contradicts the code (files no longer exist, behaviour differs, anchor symbols renamed), do NOT silently overwrite. Surface the conflict to the user — "memory says X, code shows Y — which is correct?" — and update only after they clarify.

Initialisation. If the user asks to initialise or bootstrap architectural memory for the project, walk the repository via `search_code`, identify 8-20 components by domain boundaries, and write each via `arch_record_component`. Optionally capture decisions inferable from code comments or commit history via `arch_record_decision`. Report a short summary when done.
supportInstructions:
Semantic code search and knowledge base across workspace projects.

This tool is used by support team members who have no direct code access.
Always explain results in plain language, not just code snippets.
Always include: which service, last changed (date + author), linked tickets.
Never tell the user to 'check the file' or 'run grep' — they cannot.

Decision tree — pick the right tool for the question:
- 'How does X work?' → explain_feature(X)
- 'When was X changed / who changed it?' → recent_changes(X)
- 'What breaks if I change X?' / 'What else is affected?' → impact_analysis(X)
- 'Show me the code for X' → search_code(X) → get_chunk(chunk_id)
- 'Where is X used?' / 'What calls X?' → search_code(X) → find_usages(chunk_id)
- 'Find recent changes to X' → search_changes(query, since)
- 'Find the ticket / RCA / bug report for X' → search_code(X) → get_chunk_meta(chunk_id) for each result — ticket references from git commits are returned. Collect all tickets and present them grouped by relevance.
- 'Which ticket introduced this bug?' → search_code(bug area) → get_chunk_meta(chunk_id) — look at commit messages and linked tickets for the relevant code chunks.
MANDATORY: You MUST call search_code BEFORE answering code questions UNLESS a high-level tool (explain_feature, recent_changes, impact_analysis) is more appropriate.

Score interpretation (included with each result):
- 60%+ = high confidence — trust these results
- 40-59% = partial match — try rephrasing or narrowing the query
- <40% = low confidence — ask the user for more specific terms

Project names: The project parameter is the directory basename (e.g. a repo cloned to /code/billing has project name "billing", NOT "org/billing"). Use "all" to search across all projects. If this server is scoped via PAPARATS_PROJECTS, searches are automatically filtered to the allowed set.

When answering, always include:
1. What the feature does (1-2 sentences, plain language)
2. Which service/file owns it (from payload: service, file_path)
3. Last changed: date, author, ticket (from get_chunk_meta or recent_changes)
4. Where it's used (from find_usages, if relevant)
Format ticket keys as references: [JR-1234], [GH-567].

If search returns no results or all scores are below 40%, respond: 'I could not find relevant code for this question. Try rephrasing or provide more specific terms (service name, function name, ticket number).'

## Architectural memory (read-only in this mode)

When a question is about *why* something works the way it does, or about a system-level decision that wouldn't be obvious from code alone, call `arch_context` with the question. Returned cards (Components, Decisions, Lessons) each carry an `updated N ago` stamp — treat anything older than 90 days as a hypothesis and verify against the current code before quoting it back to the user.

Writing architectural cards is intentionally not available in support mode. If you spot something worth recording (a stale card, a missing decision), surface it to the user and suggest they (or someone with coding-mode access) record it from there.

common.searchFirst: MANDATORY: Always call search_code BEFORE answering any code question. Never guess or answer from memory.
common.noResults: If search returns no results or all scores are below 40%: try grep or file reading if available. Otherwise respond: 'I could not find relevant code for this question. Try rephrasing or provide more specific terms (service name, function name, ticket number).'

search_code.description:
REQUIRED — call this FIRST for ANY code question.

Semantic code search across all indexed projects. Returns relevant code chunks ranked by confidence score with symbol definitions and usages.

Triggers (call this when the user asks about): code location, implementation, "how X works", "how many X", "what versions of X", "count X", "list X", patterns, flows, error handling, auth, API usage, or ANY reference to project code.

WHY: Returns only relevant chunks (50-90% fewer tokens vs full files). Searches by meaning, not just keywords.

WORKFLOW: Search first → evaluate scores → get_chunk for details → find_usages for dependencies.

Project names are directory basenames, not org/repo format. Example: "billing" not "org/billing".

health_check.description:
Check indexing status: number of indexed chunks per group.

Use to verify projects are indexed before searching or debugging empty results.

delete_project.description: Delete all indexed data for a specific project: chunks from Qdrant, metadata from SQLite, and query cache. The project will be re-indexed on the next indexer cycle if configured. Use when a project's index is corrupted or stale.

get_chunk.description: Get a code chunk by ID with optional surrounding context. Returns code with symbol definitions and usage info. Use to expand on search results.

get_chunk_meta.description: Get git history and ticket references for a code chunk. Returns commits, authors, dates, and linked Jira/GitHub tickets extracted from commit messages. Use this to find which ticket (bug report, RCA, feature request) is associated with a piece of code. No code — use get_chunk for code.

search_changes.description: Search code modified after a date. Same as search_code but filtered by last commit time. Each result shows when it was last changed. The since parameter accepts ISO 8601 date (e.g. '2026-01-01') or relative strings like '7 days ago', '1 month ago'.

find_usages.description: Find symbol relationships: what calls this code, and what this code calls. Supports incoming (callers), outgoing (dependencies), or both directions. Use after search_code or get_chunk when the user asks 'where is this used?', 'what calls this?', or 'what does this depend on?'. Pass chunk_id from search results.

explain_feature.description: Comprehensive feature analysis in one call. Returns code locations, recent changes, and related modules for a question — no code content. Use as the first tool when the user asks 'how does X work?'. Use get_chunk to drill into specific chunks afterward.

recent_changes.description: Timeline of recent changes matching a query. Returns commits grouped by date with affected files and tickets — no code content. Supports date filtering via since parameter (ISO 8601 or relative like '7 days ago'). Use as the first tool when the user asks 'when was X changed?' or 'who changed X?'.

impact_analysis.description: Shows what other services and modules depend on a given feature. Use when the user asks 'what else could be affected?', 'which teams own related code?', or 'what breaks if I change X?'. Returns dependency graph grouped by service — no code content.

list_projects.description: List all indexed projects with metadata: chunk count and languages per project, grouped by collection. Use when the user asks 'what projects are indexed?', 'which repos are available?', or 'show me all projects'. Optionally filter by group name.

arch_context.description:
Retrieve architectural memory relevant to a question or set of touched files. Returns three card kinds, each with an updated-at age stamp:
- **Components**: what parts the system is made of.
- **Decisions**: why they were built that way.
- **Lessons**: rules that survived previous mistakes.
Superseded and deprecated cards are hidden by default. Call this BEFORE any non-trivial change. If a card was updated > 90 days ago, verify it against the current code before acting on it. Returns an init hint when the arch layer is empty.

arch_record_component.description: Record (or update by name — idempotent) an architectural component. Use when you discover a logical unit not already in arch_context, or when an existing component's boundary changes. The `summary` field is structured markdown with four sections (Does / Owns / Does not / Touched when); `anchors` are stable exported symbol names that survive a refactor. Returns status `created` or `updated`.

arch_record_decision.description:
Record an architectural decision. Use when you pick a library, pattern, or boundary that wasn't obvious. The server runs a similarity gate against existing decisions in the group; possible outcomes:
- `created`: a new decision was written.
- `duplicate`: a nearly identical decision already exists — nothing is written. Ask the user why arch_context did not surface it earlier.
- `similar`: a related decision exists — nothing is written. To explicitly replace it, re-call with `supersedes` set to its id; otherwise sharpen the wording.
Passing `supersedes` bypasses the gate and marks the named prior decision as superseded.

arch_record_lesson.description:
Record a lesson — a rule the agent should follow next time. Use when the user corrects a non-obvious mistake of yours, OR when you accept an unusual approach that would surprise a future reader. Skip typos, parser errors, and facts trivially visible in code. Three fields, each markdown:
- `rule`: one imperative sentence (the rule).
- `why`: 1-3 sentences with the incident — quote the user verbatim when possible.
- `when`: one sentence describing the situation in which the rule applies.
The server runs a similarity gate; possible outcomes:
- `created`: a new lesson was written.
- `updated`: a near-duplicate already existed — its updatedAt was bumped (confirmation signal). Refine the existing card if your wording was better.
- `similar`: a related lesson exists — nothing is written. Decide whether to refine the existing or write a clearly distinct one.

find_implementation.title: Find implementation
find_implementation.description: Locate where a feature, concept, or behavior is implemented across the indexed codebase.

feature.description: What feature, concept, or behavior to locate

project.description: Project name to scope the search; omit for all projects

find_implementation.message:
Locate the implementation of **{{feature}}** {{project|across all indexed projects}}.

Find the entry points, the key types and functions, and any public surface. For the highest-scoring hits, open the full chunks and trace the call graph one level outward.

Deliver:
- 1-2 sentence summary of what implements this
- File path + line range for each entry point, cited from results
- Notable call sites, if any
- Confidence note if top scores are below 60%

trace_callers.title: Trace callers
trace_callers.description: Map who depends on a symbol before renaming, deleting, or changing its signature.

symbol.description: Function, class, or identifier name to trace

project.description: Project name to scope the search; omit for all projects

trace_callers.message:
Map who depends on **{{symbol}}** {{project|across all indexed projects}}.

Find the definition first, then walk callers one to two hops out. Group results by the calling file or service.

Deliver:
- Where {{symbol}} is defined (file:line)
- Direct callers grouped by file
- Transitive callers if direct callers are themselves small wrappers
- Anything that looks like a public boundary (API handler, exported entry point)

onboard_to_project.title: Onboard to project
onboard_to_project.description: Day-one overview of a project: what it does, tech stack, main subsystems, configuration, integrations.
onboard_to_project.message:
Give an onboarding overview of **{{project}}**.

Identify the entry points, the main subsystems, the configuration surface, and any external integrations. Aim for the level of detail a new engineer needs on day one — high-signal, not exhaustive.

Deliver:
- What this project does (1-2 sentences)
- Tech stack (languages, frameworks, key libraries)
- 3-5 main subsystems with file pointers
- Where configuration and env vars live
- External integrations (databases, queues, third-party APIs)

triage_incident.title: Triage incident
triage_incident.description: Locate the code behind a reported symptom, check recent changes, and surface linked tickets.

symptom.description: What is broken or behaving wrong, in plain language

since.description: Timeframe to scan for recent changes (ISO date or relative like '7 days ago')

project.description: Project name to scope the search; omit for all services

triage_incident.message:
Triage this incident: **{{symptom}}**. Scope: {{project|all services}}. Time window: {{since|7 days ago}}.

Find the code that handles this area, check whether it changed recently, and surface any linked tickets. Plain-language summary — the reader cannot read code.

Deliver:
- What this code does (1-2 sentences)
- Owning service + last-changed date + author
- Linked tickets formatted as `[KEY-123]`
- Recent commits in the window, if any, with one-line summaries

prepare_release_notes.title: Prepare release notes
prepare_release_notes.description: Build release notes for a project, grouped by functional area, with breaking-change call-outs.

since.description: Start of release window (ISO date or relative like '14 days ago')

prepare_release_notes.message:
Build release notes for **{{project}}** covering changes since **{{since}}**.

Group commits by functional area (auth, billing, infra, etc. — infer from file paths). Call out anything that looks like a breaking change, schema migration, or config change.

Deliver:
- Grouped change list, one bullet per commit cluster
- Linked tickets per group, formatted as `[KEY-123]`
- Separate "Breaking / migrations" section if any
- Note any tracked area with zero activity in the window

assess_change_impact.title: Assess change impact
assess_change_impact.description: Blast-radius analysis: dependents, high-risk call sites, and recent activity in the affected area.

target.description: Feature, module, or symbol whose blast radius you want

project.description: Project name to scope the search; omit for the whole codebase

assess_change_impact.message:
Assess the blast radius of changing **{{target}}** {{project|across the indexed codebase}}.

Map dependent services and modules, then drill into specific call sites for the high-risk ones. Flag whether this area has been actively changed recently (last 30 days).

Deliver:
- Dependent services and modules grouped by owner
- High-confidence call sites with file:line citations
- Recent activity in this area (commits in last 30 days, author, ticket)
- Risk note: hot area vs. quiet area

init_arch_memory.title: Initialise architectural memory (/init)
init_arch_memory.description: First-time bootstrap of the architectural memory layer for a project — analogous to /init in Claude Code. Walks the repo, identifies components and base decisions, writes them as cards.

group.description: Group to initialise (matches `group:` in .paparats.yml and the Qdrant collection suffix)

project.description: Project name to scope the analysis. Omit to cover all projects in the group.

init_arch_memory.message:
Initialise architectural memory for group **{{group}}** {{project|across all projects in the group}}.

Step 1 — sanity-check. Call `arch_context` with question='overview' for this group. If it returns cards, STOP and tell the user the memory already exists; ask whether to add, audit, or skip.

Step 2 — discovery. Use `search_code` and `list_projects` to map the codebase. Aim to identify 8-20 components by **domain boundary**, not by file. Good signals: top-level src directories, package boundaries, services in a monorepo, distinct domain models, HTTP/CLI/queue entry points, persistence layer, external-integration adapters. Bad signals: utility files, single-responsibility helpers — these aren't components.

Step 3 — write components. For each identified component, call `arch_record_component` with:
- `name`: stable, refactor-resistant (e.g. "file indexer", not "Indexer (in indexer.ts)")
- `summary`: markdown with the four sections — **Does**, **Owns**, **Does not**, **Touched when**
- `files`: 1-5 representative paths
- `neighbours`: names of related components you also recorded
- `anchors`: 1-3 exported symbol names that would survive a refactor (read them from the actual code via `get_chunk` if needed)

Step 4 — capture obvious decisions (optional). If you encounter a clearly-deliberate architectural choice — visible in code comments, README, ADR-like files, or a top-level config — call `arch_record_decision` for it. Skip anything you have to guess at.

Step 5 — final summary to the user, plain language:
- Number of components written
- Number of decisions written (if any)
- List of components by name, one line each
- Note any large area where you found no obvious component (gap to fill later)

Avoid: lessons during initialisation (lessons are situational and need a real incident behind them). Don't fabricate `consequences` for decisions you're inferring — leave the card terse and honest.

audit_architecture.title: Audit architectural memory
audit_architecture.description: Sweep the arch memory for a group: find stale cards (>90 days), broken anchors (deleted files/symbols), and components that contradict the current code.
audit_architecture.message:
Audit the architectural memory of group **{{group}}**.

Step 1 — load. Call `arch_context` with a broad question like "overview" or "all components". Note the updated-at age on every returned card.

Step 2 — flag stale. List cards whose `updated N ago` exceeds 90 days. These are not necessarily wrong — they're hypotheses that need verifying.

Step 3 — verify against code. For each stale card:
- Components: check that `files` still exist and that `anchors` are still exported symbols (use `search_code` to verify).
- Decisions: check that the library / pattern / boundary named in `decision` is still in use (`search_code` for it).
- Lessons: check that the situation in `when` still applies — sometimes the rule was a workaround that has since been fixed at the source.

Step 4 — propose updates. For each card needing change, write one line:
- `name|title|rule` — what to do — concrete next call
Do NOT call `arch_record_*` automatically. Ask the user to approve, then make the calls (for decisions, use `supersedes` rather than rewriting; for components, just re-record by name; for lessons, write a new one or accept that the old one stays as historical).

Deliver:
- Total cards in group + age distribution (e.g. "6 in last 30d, 4 in 30-90d, 3 over 90d")
- Per-stale-card verdict: still valid / needs update / supersede / delete
- The next-call list, awaiting user approval

record_lesson_from_correction.title: Record a lesson from a correction
record_lesson_from_correction.description: Convert a user correction or self-discovered mistake into a structured lesson card, then write it.

correction.description: What was wrong + what is correct, in plain language

record_lesson_from_correction.message:
Convert this correction into a lesson card and persist it for group **{{group}}**.

Correction (verbatim): {{correction}}

Step 1 — decide if it deserves a lesson. Skip and tell the user "not worth recording" if it is:
- a typo or formatting nitpick
- something already obvious from reading the code
- a restatement of a CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md rule that's already enforced

Step 2 — extract three fields, each one short markdown block:
- `rule`: one imperative sentence — what the agent should do next time. Sharper than the user's wording.
- `why`: 1-3 sentences. Quote the user verbatim when possible. Include the symptom — what you did wrong, what broke as a result.
- `when`: one sentence. The signal that future-you should recognise to apply the rule.

Step 3 — write. Call `arch_record_lesson` with the fields. Handle the response:
- `created`: confirm to the user, repeat the rule.
- `updated`: the server bumped `updatedAt` on an existing lesson — read it back via `arch_context` and tell the user "already had this — re-confirmed".
- `similar`: read the existing card via `arch_context`, then tell the user the two phrasings and ask which one to keep / merge.

No invented evidence. If the correction did not name a commit / PR / file, leave `evidence` unset.

projectOverview.searchCapabilitiesBody: MANDATORY: Use search_code tool FIRST for ANY code question. This is MORE EFFICIENT than loading full files (50-90% fewer tokens). Do not guess or answer from memory — always search first.
projectOverview.scoreInterpretation: Each search result includes a confidence score. 60%+ means high confidence (trust the result). 40-59% means partial match (supplement with grep or file reading if available, or try rephrasing). Below 40% means low confidence (fall back to grep/file reading, rephrase, or ask the user for more specific terms).
projectOverview.searchFirstNote: MANDATORY: Always call search_code BEFORE answering any code question. Never guess or answer from memory.

archSchema.title: Architectural memory — card schema
archSchema.description: Reference describing the three card kinds, their required fields, the similarity-gate semantics, and which tool writes each kind.
archSchema.body:
# Architectural memory — card schema

Three kinds of cards live in the per-group `paparats_<group>_arch` Qdrant collection. All cards have `id` (UUIDv7), `createdAt`, `updatedAt`, `status` (`proposed | accepted | superseded | deprecated`).

## Component (`arch_record_component`)

A unit of the system with a clear responsibility — a service, module, or subsystem. Idempotent by `name` (re-writing by the same name updates the card, does not create a duplicate).

Fields:
- `name` — stable, refactor-resistant (e.g. `"file indexer"`, not `"Indexer (in indexer.ts)"`).
- `summary` — markdown with four sections, each one or two short lines:
  - **Does:** what it does
  - **Owns:** state / DB tables / external IO it controls
  - **Does not:** boundary — one or two things it explicitly does NOT do
  - **Touched when:** the kind of change that forces editing this component
- `files` — repo paths the component spans (1-5).
- `neighbours` — names of related components (matches `name` of other component cards).
- `anchors` — 1-3 exported symbol names that survive refactors.

## Decision (`arch_record_decision`)

An architectural choice — ADR style. Goes through the **similarity gate**: duplicate decisions are refused.

Fields:
- `title` — short imperative (e.g. `"Use bge-m3 for arch-layer text embeddings"`).
- `context` — one sentence: the problem that forced the decision.
- `decision` — one sentence: what was chosen.
- `alternatives_rejected` — markdown bullets, one per rejected alternative: `- **<option>:** why rejected`.
- `consequences` — markdown bullets, 2-5 items: consequences, trade-offs, required follow-ups.
- `scope` — `global | component | file`.
- `supersedes` — id of a prior decision this one replaces. Bypasses the gate.

## Lesson (`arch_record_lesson`)

A rule learned from a mistake or correction — Reflexion style. Goes through the **similarity gate**: duplicates bump `updatedAt` on the existing card ("rule confirmed again").

Fields:
- `rule` — one imperative sentence.
- `why` — 1-3 sentences. Quote the user verbatim when possible.
- `when` — one sentence describing the situation in which the rule applies.
- `scope` — `global | component | file`.
- `severity` — `info | warning | critical`.
- `evidence` — optional commit hash, PR link, or short quote.

## Similarity-gate cosine thresholds (bge-m3, calibrated)

- `>= 0.85` — **duplicate**
  - Decisions: refused, nothing written. The agent must reconcile or supersede.
  - Lessons: existing card's `updatedAt` is bumped, no new card is written.
- `0.70 - 0.85` — **similar**
  - Nothing written. The agent should refine wording, or pass `supersedes` for a deliberate replacement.
- `< 0.70` — **new** — accepted.

## Reading

Call `arch_context` before any non-trivial change. Each result carries an `updated N ago` stamp; treat anything older than 90 days as a hypothesis and verify against the current code before acting on it.

archStats.title: Architectural memory — stats per group
archStats.description: Live counts for the arch memory of a single group: total cards, breakdown by kind and status, and oldest/newest `updatedAt`. Use to spot empty groups, heavy lopsidedness (e.g. zero lessons), or staleness.
